title: What is an Academic Advisor and How Often Should I See Them?
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You were assigned an academic advisor during orientation. You might have met them once. You might not remember their name. They sent you an email at some point that you probably did not open. You figured you would reach out when you had a problem. That approach works until you are a senior and discover you are missing a required course that is only offered in the spring, and now graduation is delayed by a full year. Your academic advisor is one of the most valuable resources on campus, but only if you use them strategically. Here is what they actually do and how often you should be meeting with them.

What an Academic Advisor Actually Does

An academic advisor is not just a course scheduler. They help you understand degree requirements, but they also help you think about your academic trajectory. They can tell you which courses to take in which sequence. They know which professors are retiring next year and which new programs are launching. They know about research opportunities, study abroad programs, and internship options that fit your goals. They can connect you to other resources on campus that you did not know existed.

Advisors also serve as advocates. If you need to petition for an exception to a policy, your advisor can guide you through the process. If you are struggling academically, your advisor can help you develop a recovery plan. If you are considering changing majors, your advisor can help you understand the implications for your graduation timeline. They are not just a bureaucratic checkpoint. They are a strategic partner in your education.

How Often Should You Meet?

At minimum, once per semester. This is the bare minimum required at most schools before you can register for classes. You will meet, review your progress, and get clearance to enroll in courses for the following term.

But minimum is not optimal. Meet with your advisor at least twice per semester. Once during the first month to discuss your goals for the term and identify any potential challenges. Once before registration opens to plan your courses for the following semester. If you are considering a major change, applying to graduate school, or planning to study abroad, schedule additional meetings to discuss those specific topics. Your advisor cannot help you with decisions they do not know you are making.

How to Prepare for an Advising Meeting

Do not show up to an advising meeting without preparation. Your advisor can only work with the information you give them. Before the meeting, review your degree requirements. Look at the courses you still need. Think about what you want to accomplish in the coming semester. Write down your questions. Bring a proposed course schedule, even if it is rough. The meeting will be more productive if you arrive with a starting point.

Questions to ask your advisor: Are there any courses I am missing that could delay my graduation? Are there electives you recommend for someone with my interests? Are there research or internship opportunities in my department that I should know about? What resources on campus could help with the challenges I am currently facing? If I am considering graduate school, what should I be doing now to prepare?

The Mistakes Students Make

The biggest mistake is waiting until a problem is urgent. Your advisor cannot fix a graduation requirement you missed if you discover it during your final semester. They cannot help you switch majors efficiently if you have already taken two years of courses in the wrong department. They cannot write you a meaningful recommendation letter if they only met you once for fifteen minutes during registration. Build the relationship before you need it.

Another mistake is treating your advisor like a personal assistant who will build your schedule for you. Your education is your responsibility. Your advisor can guide you, but they cannot make decisions on your behalf. Come to meetings prepared to participate actively in planning your own path.

Some students assume their advisor knows everything about every policy and program. Advisors are knowledgeable, but they are not omniscient. If you have a specific question about a niche program or an unusual situation, do your own research first. Your advisor can then help you interpret what you have found and connect you with the right people.

What If Your Advisor Is Not a Good Fit

Sometimes the assigned advisor is not the right person for you. They might be unresponsive. They might specialize in an area far from your interests. They might give advice that feels dismissive or unhelpful. You can request a different advisor. Contact the academic advising office or your department chair and explain your situation. Most schools will accommodate a change, especially if you can articulate why a different advisor would better serve your goals. This is your education. You are allowed to advocate for the support you need.

The Bottom Line

Your academic advisor is a strategic partner in your education. Meet with them at least twice per semester. Prepare for meetings by reviewing your requirements and writing down questions. Build the relationship before you need it. If the fit is not right, request a change. The students who use their advisors effectively are not the ones in academic crisis. They are the ones who stay ahead of requirements, discover opportunities early, and graduate on time with a plan.
